title: Comparing Two Populations Using Measures of Center

steps.instruction: Class A: mean score = 72, median = 70. Class B: mean score = 82, median = 83.
steps.work: Class A center ≈ 71. Class B center ≈ 82.5.
steps.explanation: Use mean or median (or both) to describe the typical value for each group.

steps.instruction: Which class performed better on average?
steps.work: Class B (mean 82 vs 72) scored about 10 points higher on average.
steps.explanation: The difference in means tells us how far apart the typical values are.

steps.instruction: Class A MAD = 8. Class B MAD = 5.
steps.work: Difference (10) is greater than both MADs, suggesting a meaningful difference.
steps.explanation: If the gap between means is large relative to the variability, the difference is likely real.

title: Using Visual Overlap to Compare Populations

steps.instruction: Group X: median 40, IQR 10. Group Y: median 55, IQR 12.
steps.work: Group X box: 35 to 45. Group Y box: 49 to 61.
steps.explanation: The box (Q1 to Q3) shows where the middle 50% of each group falls.

steps.work: Group X box: 35-45. Group Y box: 49-61. No overlap.
steps.explanation: No overlap means the groups are clearly different.

steps.instruction: The lack of overlap suggests a meaningful difference between groups.
steps.work: Group Y values are consistently higher than Group X values.
steps.explanation: When boxes don't overlap, the difference is almost certainly meaningful.
